harkness_discussion_prompts.title: The Fourth of July: Baldwin and the Politics of Speech
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are an LLM writing a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for a high school English class. Your output must be a transcript/play script, not notes, not an outline, and not a summary. Write the conversation as if it happened around a Harkness table in a boarding school classroom.

COURSE / SCHOOL CONTEXT
- School: The Lawrenceville School
- Context: boarding school, grades 9-12, strong readers, discussion level approximates an undergraduate seminar
- Course: EN423 Essay Writing
- Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. The second half of the course emphasizes revision. The final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks.
- Course tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy and used as a core pedagogy at the Lawrenceville School.

How it works:
- Students sit around an oval or rectangular Harkness table so everyone can see everyone else.
- The teacher sits at the table as an equal presence but does NOT moderate like a traditional classroom and does NOT call on students.
- Students drive the conversation.
- Students should reference the text directly, with books open on the table, and cite passages, page numbers, or line references.
- Students should build on, challenge, and extend each other’s ideas.
- Students should ask follow-up questions of each other, not the teacher.
- Students should tolerate productive silence; not every pause needs filling.
- Students should disagree respectfully and use textual evidence.
- The teacher opens with one rich, text-anchored question and then steps back.
- The teacher may intervene only 2-3 times to redirect a stalled discussion, push deeper, or surface a neglected thread, never to give the answer.
- The teacher closes with a synthesis move that helps the group name what they discovered together.

What makes a good Harkness question:
- It is text-anchored.
- It is genuinely contested.
- It is generative.
- It is accessible to everyone but not exhaustible quickly.
- It avoids yes/no questions and obvious right answers.

Typical discussion arc:
1. Entry — students orient to the question and offer initial readings.
2. Exploration — competing interpretations surface and evidence is marshalled.
3. Complication — tensions, contradictions, or layered meanings emerge.
4. Synthesis — the group begins to articulate a collective, not necessarily unanimous, insight.

SPECIFIC TEXT / PASSAGE
Text: James Baldwin, “Notes of a Native Son”
Focus passage: the essay’s reflection on Baldwin’s father’s death, the Harlem riot, and the essay’s closing meditation on love, rage, and inheritance.

Because no pre-sourced passage is provided, include the following verbatim excerpt(s) in the prompt so the students in the dialogue have text to cite. Use these quotes as the textual basis for the discussion and cite them naturally in the conversation:

1. “One writes out of one thing only — one’s own experience.”
2. “People are trapped in history and history is trapped in them.”
3. “The most dangerous creation of any society is the man who has nothing to lose.”
4. “Love takes off the masks that we fear we cannot live without and know we cannot live within.”
5. “I was not born in Jerusalem but I was born black and I was born in the United States, and this has made me what I am.”
6. “There is no reason for me to attempt to be what I am not, but there is every reason for me to try to know who I am.”

Your task is to write a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness table discussion as a transcript/play script based on this text and these quotes.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE DIALOGUE SCRIPT
- Use 6-8 named students with first names only, such as Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Noor, Lucas, Priya, Aiden.
- Give each student a distinct voice and perspective.
- Include the TEACHER, but the teacher should only open the discussion with one rich question, then step back, then intervene only 2-3 times to redirect or deepen, and then close with a synthesis move.
- The students must quote or paraphrase Baldwin directly in their dialogue, including page numbers or line/paragraph references if helpful. Since no edition is specified, students may refer to the quote text as quoted above, or say “in the section where Baldwin writes…”
- The conversation must show students building on each other’s ideas, at least one genuine interpretive disagreement, moments of productive silence or uncertainty, and one student changing their mind or refining their position.
- The conversation should follow a natural Harkness arc: entry/orientation, exploration of competing readings, complication/tension, and movement toward synthesis.
- Include stage directions in brackets for key moments, such as [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], [long silence], [leans back], etc.
- The dialogue should feel like real high school students who are smart and engaged but still teenagers — not like professors. Make the voices natural and conversational.
- The teacher’s opening question should invite disagreement and close reading, not summary.
- Do NOT write an essay, a lesson plan, or bullet points. Write only the dialogue script.

OUTPUT FORMAT
- Produce a single continuous transcript/play script.
- Label speakers by name, including TEACHER.
- No introductory explanation outside the script.
- End with a teacher synthesis remark that does not “solve” the text but names the collective insight the group reached.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: Self-Reliance and the Problem of Individualism
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are an LLM writing a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for a high school English class at a boarding school. The response must be an actual transcript/play script of students talking around a Harkness table, not notes, not an outline, and not a teacher’s lesson plan.

COURSE / SCHOOL CONTEXT
- School: The Lawrenceville School
- Context: boarding school, grades 9-12, strong readers, undergraduate seminar level discussion
- Course: EN423 Essay Writing
- Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. The second half emphasizes revision. The final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks.
- Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy and used at Lawrenceville.

Essential features:
- Students sit around an oval or rectangular Harkness table where everyone can see everyone else.
- The teacher is present but does not direct the conversation like a moderator.
- Students lead the discussion, referring directly to the text with books open on the table.
- Students quote, cite, question, disagree, and build on each other.
- They ask each other follow-up questions, not the teacher.
- Silence is allowed and can be productive.
- Disagreement should be respectful and text-based.
- The teacher opens with a single rich, text-anchored question and then steps back.
- The teacher intervenes only a few times, and only to push deeper, redirect when stuck, or surface a neglected thread.
- The teacher closes by synthesizing what the class has collectively discovered.

What makes a good Harkness question:
- Text-anchored
- Generative
- Contestable
- Accessible but not easy
- Not reducible to yes/no or one correct answer

Discussion arc:
1. Entry — initial responses and orientation.
2. Exploration — evidence and competing interpretations.
3. Complication — contradictions and tensions emerge.
4. Synthesis — the group identifies a larger insight without fully closing the meaning.

SPECIFIC TEXT / PASSAGE
Text: Ralph Waldo Emerson, “Self-Reliance”
Focus passage: the essay’s argument about self-trust, conformity, and consistency, especially the sections that include the following lines.

Embed these quotes verbatim and use them as the textual basis for student citations:

1. “Whoso would be a man must be a nonconformist.”
2. “Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string.”
3. “A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds, adored by little statesmen and philosophers and divines.”
4. “To be great is to be misunderstood.”
5. “Nothing can bring you peace but yourself.”
6. “The power which resides in him is new in nature, and none but he knows what that is which he can do, nor does he know until he has tried.”

Write a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness discussion based on this text and these quotations.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE DIALOGUE SCRIPT
- Include 6-8 named students with first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Noor, Lucas, Priya, Aiden.
- Each student must have a distinct voice and perspective.
- Include the TEACHER, who opens with one rich question and then mostly stays out, intervening only 2-3 times to redirect, deepen, or surface a neglected thread, and then closes with a synthesis move.
- The students must quote or closely reference Emerson directly in the conversation, ideally with textual citations such as “in the line where Emerson says…” or “when he writes ‘Trust thyself…’”.
- Include at least one genuine interpretive disagreement.
- Include moments of productive silence or uncertainty.
- Include one student revising or changing their mind during the discussion.
- The discussion should move naturally through entry/orientation, exploration, complication, and synthesis.
- Include stage directions in brackets for key moments: [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], [long silence], [smiles], etc.
- Make the conversation sound like smart high school students, not professors.
- Do not write an essay, bullet list, or summary. Write only the script.

OUTPUT FORMAT
- Present the result as a continuous transcript with speaker labels.
- Use TEACHER as a speaker label only when the teacher speaks.
- The final line should be a teacher synthesis that captures the class’s shared insight without resolving every tension.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: The Immigrant City and the Ethical Witness
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are an LLM tasked with generating a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for an English class. The final answer must be an actual transcript/play script, not notes, not an outline, and not analysis.

COURSE / SCHOOL CONTEXT
- School: The Lawrenceville School
- Context: boarding school, grades 9-12, strong readers, discussion approximates an undergraduate seminar
- Course: EN423 Essay Writing
- Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. The second half emphasizes revision. The final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks.
- Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method used at Lawrenceville and developed at Phillips Exeter Academy.

Key principles:
- Students sit in a Harkness oval or rectangle so all can see each other.
- The teacher sits as an equal presence but does not lecture or direct the conversation.
- Students drive the discussion and speak to one another.
- Students reference the text directly, with open books and quoted passages.
- Students ask each other questions and challenge one another respectfully with evidence.
- Silence is acceptable and can be productive.
- The teacher opens with one rich question and then steps back.
- The teacher may intervene only 2-3 times to redirect a stalled conversation, deepen the inquiry, or surface an overlooked thread.
- The teacher closes by synthesizing what the class seems to have discovered together.

What makes a good Harkness question:
- Anchored in the text
- Open to multiple interpretations
- Genuinely contestable
- Rich enough for a 20-30 minute conversation
- Not yes/no, not “what is the theme,” not something with one correct answer

Discussion arc:
1. Entry — first impressions and orienting claims.
2. Exploration — evidence and competing readings.
3. Complication — tensions, contradictions, ethical questions.
4. Synthesis — a provisional shared insight emerges.

SPECIFIC TEXT / PASSAGE
Text: Joan Didion, “Goodbye to All That”
Focus passage: Didion’s reflections on New York, innocence, disillusionment, and the emotional force of departure and memory.

Embed the following quotes verbatim so students can cite them in the dialogue:

1. “It is easy to see the beginnings of things, and harder to see the ends.”
2. “Somewhere along the way, the city had a way of making you feel that everything would happen, if only you stayed long enough.”
3. “You could easily see how it might be a place where you could lose yourself.”
4. “You remember only what you want to remember. You know only what you want to know.”
5. “I can remember no time in New York when I was not either somewhat in love or somewhat depressed.”
6. “The willingness to accept responsibility for one’s own life is the source from which self-respect springs.”

Write a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness discussion based on this text and these quotations.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE DIALOGUE SCRIPT
- Include 6-8 named students with first names only, such as Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Noor, Lucas, Priya, Aiden.
- Give each student a distinct voice and perspective.
- Include the TEACHER, who asks one rich opening question, then largely withdraws, intervening only 2-3 times to redirect or deepen, and then closing with a synthesis move.
- Students must quote and reference Didion directly in the conversation.
- Include at least one genuine interpretive disagreement about the tone or ethical stance of the essay.
- Include moments of productive silence or uncertainty.
- Include one student changing their mind or refining a claim after hearing others.
- The conversation must move naturally through entry/orientation, exploration, complication, and synthesis.
- Include stage directions in brackets such as [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], [long silence], [looks down], etc.
- Make the voices sound like real high school students — smart, thoughtful, a little informal, not like professors.
- Do not write an essay, a list, or a teacher guide. Write only the dialogue script.

OUTPUT FORMAT
- Provide a single transcript with speaker labels.
- TEACHER should speak only at the opening, 2-3 brief interventions, and the closing synthesis.
- End with a teacher remark that names the class’s collective insight without flattening disagreement.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: The Rhetoric of Satire in Swift’s Proposal
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are an LLM generating a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for a high school English class. The final answer must be a transcript/play script of a student-led seminar around a Harkness table, not a summary, not a lesson plan, and not bullet points.

COURSE / SCHOOL CONTEXT
- School: The Lawrenceville School
- Context: boarding school, grades 9-12, strong readers, seminar-level discussion
- Course: EN423 Essay Writing
- Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. The second half emphasizes revision. The final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks.
- Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy and used at Lawrenceville.

Core features:
- Students sit in a Harkness oval or rectangle so everyone can see everyone.
- The teacher sits with the group but does not dominate or call on students.
- Students lead the conversation, using the text directly.
- Students quote, paraphrase, and cite passages while building on one another’s ideas.
- Students ask each other questions, not the teacher.
- Silence is fine when useful.
- Students should disagree respectfully and support claims with textual evidence.
- The teacher opens with one rich question and then steps back.
- The teacher may intervene 2-3 times only to redirect, deepen, or surface a neglected angle.
- The teacher closes by synthesizing what the group has discovered.

What makes a good Harkness question:
- Text-based
- Open-ended and contested
- Generative enough for a long discussion
- Accessible to everyone but not easily exhausted
- Not a yes/no question and not something with a single answer

Discussion arc:
1. Entry — initial reading and orientation.
2. Exploration — students test interpretations with evidence.
3. Complication — tensions, irony, and contradictions emerge.
4. Synthesis — the group articulates a provisional shared understanding.

SPECIFIC TEXT / PASSAGE
Text: Jonathan Swift, “A Modest Proposal”
Focus passage: Swift’s satirical argument about poverty, economic exploitation, and the shocking “proposal” itself, especially the sections below.

Embed these quotes verbatim for students to cite naturally in discussion:

1. “I think it is agreed by all parties, that this prodigious number of children in the arms, or on the backs, or at the heels of their mothers, and frequently of their fathers, is in the present deplorable state of the kingdom, a very great additional grievance.”
2. “A young healthy child well nursed, is, at a year old, a most delicious, nourishing, and wholesome food, whether stewed, roasted, baked, or boiled.”
3. “I grant this food will be somewhat dear, and therefore very proper for landlords.”
4. “I have no children, by which I can propose to get a single penny.”
5. “I have already computed the charge of nursing a beggar’s child.”
6. “Therefore let no man talk to me of other expedients: Of taxing our absentees at five shillings a pound.”

Write a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness discussion centered on these quotations and the essay’s rhetoric.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE DIALOGUE SCRIPT
- Use 6-8 named students with first names only, e.g. Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Noor, Lucas, Priya, Aiden.
- Give each student a distinct voice and perspective.
- Include the TEACHER, who opens with one rich question and then mostly stays quiet, intervening only 2-3 times to redirect or deepen the conversation, and then closing with a synthesis move.
- Students must quote Swift directly and refer to the language in the discussion.
- Include a genuine interpretive disagreement about whether the essay is primarily moral outrage, cynical persuasion, or something else.
- Include moments of productive silence or uncertainty.
- Include one student revising their interpretation after hearing a peer’s evidence.
- The conversation should move through entry/orientation, exploration, complication, and synthesis.
- Include stage directions in brackets such as [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], [long silence], [raises eyebrows], etc.
- The students should sound like bright teenagers, not academic experts.
- Do not write an essay or outline. Write only the dialogue script.

OUTPUT FORMAT
- Provide a continuous transcript with speaker labels.
- Label the teacher as TEACHER.
- The final teacher line should synthesize without eliminating the discussion’s tensions.

harkness_discussion_prompts.title: Disillusionment, Irony, and the American Dream in Gatsby
harkness_discussion_prompts.prompt:
You are an LLM writing a realistic Harkness discussion DIALOGUE SCRIPT for a high school English class. The output must be an actual play-script/transcript of students discussing a literary passage around a Harkness table. Do not write notes, an essay, or a lesson plan.

COURSE / SCHOOL CONTEXT
- School: The Lawrenceville School
- Context: boarding school, grades 9-12, strong readers, undergraduate seminar-level discussion
- Course: EN423 Essay Writing
- Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. The second half emphasizes revision. The final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks.
- Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone

THE HARKNESS DISCUSSION METHOD
Harkness discussion is a student-led, collaborative inquiry method developed at Phillips Exeter Academy and used as a major pedagogy at Lawrenceville.

The method works like this:
- Students sit around a Harkness oval or rectangle so everyone can see everyone else.
- The teacher sits with the class as an equal presence but does not run the discussion.
- Students speak to each other and drive the inquiry.
- Students refer directly to the text, keeping books open on the table.
- Students quote, paraphrase, question, and challenge one another respectfully.
- Students ask follow-up questions of each other, not the teacher.
- Silence can be productive and should not always be filled.
- The teacher opens with one rich, text-anchored question and then steps back.
- The teacher may intervene only 2-3 times to redirect or deepen the conversation, or to surface an overlooked thread.
- The teacher closes with a synthesis move that names what the class discovered together.

What makes a good Harkness question:
- It is text-anchored.
- It invites multiple reasonable interpretations.
- It is generative and open-ended.
- It is accessible but not quickly exhausted.
- It avoids yes/no or one-answer framing.

Discussion arc:
1. Entry — students orient to the passage.
2. Exploration — they compare readings and marshal evidence.
3. Complication — tensions, contradictions, and implications emerge.
4. Synthesis — the group reaches a provisional shared insight.

SPECIFIC TEXT / PASSAGE
Text: F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby
Focus passage: the closing chapter and Nick’s reflective ending, especially the sections about the valley of ashes, the green light, and the famous final lines.

Embed these quotes verbatim so students can cite them directly during the discussion:

1. “Gatsby believed in the green light, the orgastic future that year by year recedes before us.”
2. “So we beat on, boats against the current, borne back ceaselessly into the past.”
3. “They were careless people, Tom and Daisy — they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness.”
4. “His dream must have seemed so close that he could hardly fail to grasp it.”
5. “Gatsby’s wonder when he first picked out the green light at the end of Daisy’s dock.”
6. “The eyes of Doctor T. J. Eckleburg are blue and gigantic — their retinas are one yard high.”

Write a realistic 20-30 minute Harkness discussion centered on these lines and the novel’s final chapter.

REQUIREMENTS FOR THE DIALOGUE SCRIPT
- Use 6-8 named students with first names only, such as Maya, James, Sofia, Ethan, Noor, Lucas, Priya, Aiden.
- Give each student a distinct voice and perspective.
- Include the TEACHER, who opens with one rich question and then mostly stays out, intervening only 2-3 times to redirect or deepen, and then closing with a synthesis move.
- Students must quote and reference the text directly in conversation, with page numbers if desired, or by chapter/line if no page numbers are available.
- Include at least one genuine interpretive disagreement, especially about whether the ending is romantic, tragic, cynical, or both.
- Include moments of productive silence or uncertainty.
- Include one student changing their mind or sharpening a claim after hearing another student.
- The discussion should move naturally through entry/orientation, exploration, complication, and synthesis.
- Include stage directions in brackets such as [pause], [flipping pages], [nodding], [turning to address another student], [long silence], [looks at book], etc.
- The students should sound like real teenagers: intelligent, curious, and conversational, not like professors.
- Do not write an essay or commentary. Write only the dialogue script.

OUTPUT FORMAT
- Present the result as a continuous transcript with speaker labels.
- The teacher may only speak at the opening, 2-3 brief interventions, and the closing synthesis.
- End with a teacher synthesis line that captures the group’s collective understanding without flattening disagreement.

essay_prompts.title: Didion and the Grammar of Grief
essay_prompts.prompt: In EN423: Essay Writing, where the course focuses on rhetorical technique, essay structure, and revision in polished nonfiction, write an 800–1000 word argumentative essay on Joan Didion’s The Year of Magical Thinking, specifically the early chapter section in which she describes the sudden death of John Gregory Dunne and the logic of her “magical” refusals. Your thesis must be this exact claim: Didion shows that grief produces a broken form of reasoning in which the mind uses repetition, denial, and ritual not to escape reality but to cling to it, so the essay’s power lies in its exact record of how language fails and persists under shock. You must quote and analyze at least these three passages: “Life changes fast. Life changes in the instant”; “The power of grief to derange the mind has in fact been exhaustively noted”; and “I needed to be alone so that he could come back.” You must also discuss her repeated attention to the hospital objects, the shoes, and the dead husband’s place at the table as evidence of grief’s compulsive literalism. Include a counterargument that directly confronts the interpretation that Didion’s thinking is irrational and simply pathological; rebut it by showing how the essay portrays magical thinking as a recognizable human attempt to preserve meaning in the face of irreparable loss. Structure the essay in six paragraphs: introduction, two body paragraphs on time and shock, two body paragraphs on denial and ritual, and a conclusion. Use a formal, literary-analytic register with careful attention to sentence-level rhetoric, and avoid generalizing about grief without tying every claim to a specific quoted passage.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: Joan Didion, "On Keeping a Notebook" — Ethos, Fragment, and Self-Invention
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Your students are capable readers who can handle undergraduate seminar-level discussion. This is EN423: Essay Writing. Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. Second half emphasizes revision. Final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks. Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone.

SESSION FOCUS: Joan Didion, "On Keeping a Notebook" (The White Album, 1979), especially the opening through the sentence "We are well advised to keep on nodding terms with the people we used to be, whether we find them attractive company or not." Focus on pages/lines corresponding to the opening 3-4 pages in common classroom editions.

Relevant passages to use verbatim in the lesson plan discussion and activities:
- "We are well advised to keep on nodding terms with the people we used to be, whether we find them attractive company or not."
- "Remember what it was to be me: that is always the point."
- "I think we are well advised to keep on nodding terms with the people we used to be."
- "It all comes back."
- "...the notebook... is the only kind of diary I have ever kept."
- "I write entirely to find out what I'm thinking, what I'm looking at, what I see and what it means."
- "I sometimes find myself reading over old notebooks and revising what I wrote."
- "We tell ourselves stories in order to live."

Write a complete, ready-to-use class session plan that fits this single class period. Include enough detail that another teacher could teach it immediately.

Use exactly the following sections and labels in your response:
A) SESSION FOCUS — the exact narrow topic with references
B) LEARNING OBJECTIVES — 3-4 observable outcomes
C) ENTRY ACTIVITY — 5-8 minutes, a specific warm-up tied to the focus
D) DIRECT INSTRUCTION / MINI-LESSON — 10 minutes, what the teacher teaches, referencing the specific passage or technique
E) CLOSE READING / DISCUSSION ACTIVITY — 20-25 minutes, the central activity with exact passages, method (Harkness, Socratic, pair-share, etc.), and 2-3 lens questions
F) ASSESSMENT / EXIT ACTIVITY — 5-10 minutes, exit ticket, written response, or similar
G) DIFFERENTIATION NOTES — one scaffolding suggestion and one extension suggestion
H) MATERIALS LIST — texts, handouts, multimedia needed

Make the plan specific to this essay and this session focus only. Do not turn it into a unit plan. Keep the timing granular and the discussion questions tightly tied to the quoted passages.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: James Baldwin, "Notes of a Native Son" — Parentheses, Memory, and Argument
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Your students are capable readers who can handle undergraduate seminar-level discussion. This is EN423: Essay Writing. Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. Second half emphasizes revision. Final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks. Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone.

SESSION FOCUS: James Baldwin, "Notes of a Native Son" (1955), focusing on the section in which Baldwin describes his father's death and the Harlem riot, especially the opening pages and the meditation on the funeral/riot juxtaposition. Use common classroom editions and refer to the opening 4-6 pages or the first several paragraphs where the juxtaposition is established.

Relevant passages to use verbatim in the lesson plan discussion and activities:
- "On the day of my father's funeral, my last sight of him was in the coffin."
- "...the day after my father's funeral, the twentieth of July, 1943, I was in a race riot in Harlem."
- "I was in a way responsible for the death of my father."
- "I was twenty-four years old when my father died."
- "People are trapped in history and history is trapped in them."
- "The city was on fire."
- "I know that what I was doing was very dangerous."
- "...the hatred I felt, the hatred I saw, the hatred I feared..."

Write a complete, ready-to-use class session plan that fits this single class period. Include enough detail that another teacher could teach it immediately.

Use exactly the following sections and labels in your response:
A) SESSION FOCUS — the exact narrow topic with references
B) LEARNING OBJECTIVES — 3-4 observable outcomes
C) ENTRY ACTIVITY — 5-8 minutes, a specific warm-up tied to the focus
D) DIRECT INSTRUCTION / MINI-LESSON — 10 minutes, what the teacher teaches, referencing the specific passage or technique
E) CLOSE READING / DISCUSSION ACTIVITY — 20-25 minutes, the central activity with exact passages, method (Harkness, Socratic, pair-share, etc.), and 2-3 lens questions
F) ASSESSMENT / EXIT ACTIVITY — 5-10 minutes, exit ticket, written response, or similar
G) DIFFERENTIATION NOTES — one scaffolding suggestion and one extension suggestion
H) MATERIALS LIST — texts, handouts, multimedia needed

Make the plan specific to this essay and this session focus only. Do not turn it into a unit plan. Keep the timing granular and the discussion questions tightly tied to the quoted passages.

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: Virginia Woolf, "Death of a Moth" — Analogy, Observation, and the Essayistic Turn
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Your students are capable readers who can handle undergraduate seminar-level discussion. This is EN423: Essay Writing. Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. Second half emphasizes revision. Final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks. Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone.

SESSION FOCUS: Virginia Woolf, "Death of a Moth" (1942), focusing on the passage where the speaker observes the moth at the window and then turns from description to meditation. Use the opening and middle sections, roughly the first 2-4 pages in common classroom editions.

Relevant passages to use verbatim in the lesson plan discussion and activities:
- "One could not help watching him."
- "He was a tiny bead of palpable energy..."
- "The moth was trying to resume his dance."
- "...a vitality... seemed to leap up and be restored."
- "The moth having righted himself now appeared to be paralyzed."
- "One expected him to fly away and resume his dance."
- "The complete disintegration of life was impossible."
- "...the true nature of life..."

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: Annie Dillard, "The Chase" — Scene, Syntax, and Escalation
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Your students are capable readers who can handle undergraduate seminar-level discussion. This is EN423: Essay Writing. Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. Second half emphasizes revision. Final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks. Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone.

SESSION FOCUS: Annie Dillard, "The Chase" (from An American Childhood, 1987), focusing on the sequence in which the children pursue the rival boys and the story builds momentum through sentence rhythm and scene shifts. Use the section in common classroom editions spanning the chase itself; refer to the episode in the neighborhood and the pursuit/retreat sequence, roughly the middle of the essay or chapter excerpt.

Relevant passages to use verbatim in the lesson plan discussion and activities:
- "We ran after them."
- "They ran away from us."
- "We chased them around the block."
- "The boys were faster than we were."
- "We wanted to catch them."
- "Our hearts were pounding."
- "We were not afraid."
- "The whole thing was like a game..."
- "The chase seemed endless."
- "...until suddenly the balance changed."

lesson_plan_prompts.title: Lesson: George Orwell, "Shooting an Elephant" — Moral Pressure and Retrospective Irony
lesson_plan_prompts.prompt:
You are an experienced English teacher designing a single 45-60 minute class session plan for Lawrenceville School, a boarding school serving grades 9-12. Your students are capable readers who can handle undergraduate seminar-level discussion. This is EN423: Essay Writing. Course description: Students read three or four exemplary essays per week from The Norton Reader and other sources, becoming familiar with rhetorical techniques. Students write a short essay approximately every week-and-a-half. Second half emphasizes revision. Final assignment is a capstone essay expanded, condensed, and revised over several weeks. Tags: essay, nonfiction, rhetoric, revision, Norton Reader, capstone.

SESSION FOCUS: George Orwell, "Shooting an Elephant" (1936), focusing on the confrontation with the elephant and the narrator’s retrospective explanation of imperial pressure. Use the central pages/section where the elephant is found, the crowd gathers, and the narrator decides to shoot.

Relevant passages to use verbatim in the lesson plan discussion and activities:
- "I was hated by large numbers of people"
- "It was a tiny incident in itself"
- "I did not want to shoot the elephant"
- "The people expected it"
- "The crowd grew very slowly"
- "I had got to shoot the elephant"
- "I could feel their two thousand wills pressing me forward"
- "When the white man turns tyrant it is his own freedom that he destroys"
- "I often wondered whether any of the others grasped that I had done it solely to avoid looking a fool"
